planting.note: Direct sow in fall on bare, moist to seasonally wet soil; will self-sow and return reliably in suitable conditions.

directions: California Goldfields is a tiny native annual that forms legendary carpets of solid golden-yellow across vernal pools, coastal grasslands, and valley floors in spring, and is one of the principal wildflowers of California superbloom events in years with ample rainfall. Scatter seed in fall on bare, moist to seasonally wet, clay-heavy or sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun, pressing seed lightly into the surface without covering. Plants self-sow prolifically and will re-establish year after year where conditions suit them, requiring no supplemental irrigation once the rains begin. This low-growing annual works beautifully as a groundcover in California native gardens or meadow plantings alongside other vernal pool species.
sources: Las Pilitas Nursery Native Plant Guide

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, lean, well-drained soil; flowers open in the morning and close by early afternoon.

directions: Common Madia is a strongly aromatic native annual tarweed with glandular-sticky, resinous stems and bright yellow, daisy-like flowers whose ray petals are marked at the base with a distinctive maroon blotch; flowers open in the morning and close by early afternoon. Native to dry, open grasslands, meadows, and chaparral margins throughout California and the Pacific Northwest, it thrives in lean, sandy or loamy, well-drained soil with no supplemental summer water once established. Direct sow seed in fall in full sun, scattering lightly and pressing into contact with the soil surface; plants germinate with winter rains and bloom from late spring into summer. The sticky resin on the stems reportedly deters deer browsing, making this an excellent, drought-tolerant choice for sunny dry garden borders in Mediterranean-climate gardens.
sources: Las Pilitas Nursery Native Plant Guide

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, lean, sandy or clay soil; best suited to California's summer-dry Mediterranean climate.

directions: Truncated Rosin Weed is a glandular-sticky, resinous native annual tarweed of dry, open foothill grasslands and chaparral margins in northern and central California, notable for its white to cream-white, 3-lobed ray flowers surrounding yellow disk florets — one of the few white-rayed tarweeds in California's flora. The strong, characteristic resinous scent of crushed foliage is typical of the tarweed tribe, and the sticky stems can trap small insects. Direct sow in fall in dry, lean, well-drained sandy or clay soil in full sun; plants germinate with winter rains and bloom from June through September with no supplemental irrigation. This species is best adapted to California's summer-dry Mediterranean climate and is well suited to dry garden naturalization and native grassland restoration plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring after last frost; this warm-season tarweed blooms through California's dry summer when few other wildflowers remain.

directions: Hayfield Tarweed is a resinous native annual of dry, open grasslands, roadsides, and disturbed hayfields throughout California's Central Valley and Coast Ranges, producing small, yellow, daisy-like composite flowers with toothed ray petals on branching, glandular-hairy stems from summer through early fall. Unlike most California native annuals, this is a warm-season species that should be direct sown in spring after the last frost rather than in fall, as it is adapted to germinate and bloom through California's long dry summers. Sow in dry, lean, well-drained, sandy to clay, slightly acidic soil in full sun with no irrigation needed after establishment. Hayfield Tarweed is a valuable late-season nectar source for native bees at a time when few other wildflowers remain in bloom in summer-dry California landscapes.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in dry, lean, sandy or gravelly soil; adapted to California's dry-season blooming cycle.

directions: Clustered Tarweed is a glandular-sticky, resinous native annual of dry, open, disturbed ground, roadsides, and grassland edges in southern California's coastal sage scrub and chaparral zones, bearing tightly clustered heads of small, yellow, 5-rayed composite flowers from summer through early fall on slender, branching, rough-textured stems. It is an ecological specialist of California's summer-dry Mediterranean climate, germinating with spring warmth and blooming through the long rainless months when it serves as a critical late-season pollen and nectar source for native bees. Direct sow in spring after last frost in dry, lean, sandy or gravelly, well-drained soil in full sun, pressing seed lightly to the surface; no supplemental irrigation is needed or desired after plants are established. This species is best suited to gardens within its native southern California range where summer drought and heat drive its bloom cycle.
